3.1 Free Energy

Thermodynamic Systems (1 of 2)
A system is any part of the universe that we choose to study and everything else is the surroundings
Systems are defined in terms of composition, temperature, pressure, and volume

Thermodynamic Systems (2 of 2)
Systems may be open, closed, or isolated
Closed systems - can exchange energy (e.g., heat or work) but not matter with surroundings
Open systems - can exchange matter and energy with surroundings
Isolated systems - can exchange neither matter nor energy with surroundings
Living systems generally operate at constant temperature and pressure

The First Law of Thermodynamics and Enthalpy
The first law of thermodynamics: Energy is conserved
In a closed system, energy can be exchanged with the surroundings in the form of work or heat
Enthalpy (H) is the heat exchanged between the system and the surroundings at constant pressure (which is the typical condition of living systems)
Measuring the Heat of a Reaction

Reversible and Irreversible Processes (1 of 2)
A reversible process is near a state of equilibrium
A process is at equilibrium when:
It is the lowest energy state of the system
The forward and reverse rates of the process are equal
Example: ice melting at

Reversible and Irreversible Processes (2 of 2)
Irreversible processes are set up far from an equilibrium state, and then proceed towards that state
Example: burning paper

The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy (1 of 3)
The second law of thermodynamics: The entropy of an isolated system will tend to increase to a maximum value
The degree of randomness or disorder of a system is measured by a function of state called the entropy (S)

The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy (2 of 3)
High entropy in the initial state (left), low entropy due to changing the system conditions (middle), and higher entropy in the final state (right) when a new equilibrium is reached

The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy (3 of 3)
Table 3.1 Examples of lower-entropy and higher-entropy states

Calculation of Keq – An Example

Equilibrium versus Homeostasis (1 of 3)
Homeostasis: set of conditions (temperature, p H, concentrations of ions and metabolites) occurring in cellular systems (living organisms)
In both cases, equilibrium and homeostasis, the concentrations of reactants and products are constant

Equilibrium versus Homeostasis (2 of 3)
However, in cellular systems, steady-state concentrations are maintained far from equilibrium values
Energy is required to maintain homeostasis!

Coupling Unfavorable and Favorable Reactions (1 of 2)
Cellular systems couple favorable and unfavorable processes to drive the unfavorable process forward

Coupling Unfavorable and Favorable Reactions (2 of 2)
This type of coupling is used to drive unfavorable chemical reactions, pump metabolites across membranes against a concentration gradient, transmit nerve impulses, contract muscles, etc.

Free Energy and Phosphoryl Group Transfer Potential (1 of 3)
The
for the hydrolysis of some cellular phosphate
compounds (e.g., phosphoenolpyruvate) is more favorable (has a more negative value) than the hydrolysis of A T P
In case of phosphoenolpyruvate (P E P) due to:
resonance stabilization of phosphate ion product
charge repulsion in reactants
tautomerization of products

Chapter 3 Summary (1 of 2)
The principles of thermodynamics explain how cell-based living systems extract energy from their environment and use it to drive cellular processes
Each of these processes must be favorable to occur
spontaneously, that is, the free energy change,
must be negative (“exergonic”) rather
than positive (“endergonic”)
Living systems operate far from equilibrium under
homeostatic conditions. Thus,
with
representing the free energy change under standard
biochemical conditions

organic phosphate compounds or concentration gradients across membranes)
